title: Encode and Decode Strings
description: Design an algorithm to encode a list of strings to a single string. The encoded string is then sent over the network and is decoded back to the original list of strings. Implement encode(strs) which encodes a list of strings to a single string, and decode(s) which decodes a single string to a list of strings. The string may contain any possible characters including special characters. Your algorithm should be generalized enough to work on any possible characters.
constraints: strs[i] contains any possible characters out of 256 valid ASCII characters
thinking_questions: If you joined strings with '#', what goes wrong when a string itself contains '#'?
What if you encoded the length of each string before it?
How do protocols like HTTP handle this same framing problem?

hints.L1: A simple delimiter breaks when strings contain that delimiter — you need encoding that unambiguously marks where each string ends regardless of its content.
hints.L2: Length-prefix encoding: encode each string as '{len}#{string}'. Since you know the length, read exactly that many characters — no ambiguity.

follow_up_questions: Why doesn't '#' alone work as delimiter? Give a concrete failing example.
What other encoding strategies could work? (escaping, non-printable delimiter, netstring format)
Where else do you see length-prefix encoding in real systems?
solution_notes: Length-prefix: '{len}#{str}'. Decode: find '#', read length, extract that many chars. O(n).
